interrogator_dossier:
# Yelp Inc. (YELP) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Six consecutive earnings calls reviewed: Q1 2025 (2025-05-08) through Q1 2026 (2026-05-08). Financials from the screener scan (FY2021–FY2025 trajectory) plus TTM/latest-quarter overrides as of 2026-03-31.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story management tells has quietly but decisively shifted from **"resilient two-sided local-ad platform"** to **"AI transformation that will reaccelerate a structurally stalling core."** That shift is the whole dossier.

- **Q1 2025** opened confidently: "8% year-over-year revenue growth and strong profitability," Services "16th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth," RR&O (Restaurants, Retail & Other) merely "challenging." AI was a feature layer (Yelp Assistant, photo recognition, answering services "coming soon"). The frame was *growth with a soft pocket*.
- By **Q2 2025** the soft pocket widened: revenue growth decelerated to **4%**, RR&O **-5%**, and even Services growth halved from 14% to **8%**. Management introduced the **data-licensing / AI-search API** narrative ("API calls up 20x over the past year… $10M annual run rate") — the first new growth driver bolted on to offset core deceleration.
- **Q3 2025**: revenue +4% again, RR&O -2%, Services +7%, ad clicks now **-11% YoY**. The DoorDash partnership (food ordering) and the Yelp Host/Receptionist voice-SaaS products are introduced. Adjusted EBITDA actually *declined* 3% YoY for the first time in the window.
- **Q4/FY2025**: the pivot is now explicit and capitalized into a three-pillar 2026 strategy — (1) conversational Yelp Assistant, (2) AI SaaS for businesses (Hatch acquisition closed, Yelp Host), (3) data licensing across the AI ecosystem (OpenAI deal signed). RR&O revenue fell **-6%** for the full year.
- **Q1 2026** is the tell: total revenue **+1% YoY**, RR&O ad revenue **-11%**, Services ad revenue **+1%** (services has now effectively stalled too), paying locations **-6% to 485k**, net income **-27% YoY**, adjusted EBITDA **-7%**. The *only* thing growing is **Other revenue +75%** (Hatch inclusion + licensing + food ordering). Management reframes the entire business around a **$250M other-revenue run-rate target by end-2028** (vs ~$116M run-rate in Q1-26).

**Growth drivers introduced then leaned on harder each quarter:** Yelp Assistant (project share 5%→15%), data licensing (OpenAI, Apple Maps, Bing, Alexa, Meta.ai), Yelp Host (1.5M-call annual run-rate by April 2026), Hatch ($34M ARR, +92%). **Quietly contracting:** the core advertising engine — paying locations have fallen every single quarter (517k→515k→512k→…→485k), ad clicks have been negative all six quarters and are accelerating downward (-3%→-7%→-11%→-10%), and the headline revenue growth rate collapsed from 8% to 1% across five quarters. The narrative has gotten *richer* (more product names, bigger TAMs) precisely as the financial base got *poorer*. That is the classic shape of a management team out-talking a decelerating core.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | Hard number | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Services… 16th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth" | Q1-25 | Services +14%, but +8% by Q2-25, +7% Q3-25, **+1% Q1-26** | **Diverges** — the growth engine the whole thesis rested on decelerated to a stall in 12 months |
| "strong profitability… margin potential" | all | Net margin 7%→12%→10%→5%; FY25 NI +10%; **Q1-26 NI -27% YoY** | **Mixed** — FY-level profitability real, but the latest quarter shows margin compression as they spend into the pivot |
| "record net revenue" | Q2/Q3-25 | True quarter-on-quarter, but FY25 rev only **+4%** and Q1-26 **+1%** | **Technically true, narratively misleading** — "records" mask a 1% growth rate |
| "disciplined expense management… flat headcount" | all | FCF margin 22%, TTM FCF $281M, P/FCF 4.7x | **Matches** — this is the genuinely strong part; the cash engine is real |
| Aggressive buyback / shrinking share count | Q4-25 | Diluted shares 86M→67M (-22%) 2021–2025; $292M repurchased FY25; **$125M in Q1-26 at $24.58** | **Matches and is the strongest pro-shareholder fact in the file** |
| "other revenue… $250M run-rate by 2028" | Q1-26 | Other rev +75% to $29M/qtr (~$116M run-rate); needs ~2.2x in <3 yrs | **Aspirational** — credible given Hatch's $34M ARR +92% and licensing, but it is replacing, not adding to, a shrinking ad base |
| EPS growth +45% 5-yr CAGR | scan | FY EPS 0.50→0.50→1.35→1.88→2.24 | **Matches, but flatters** — most of the EPS CAGR is buyback-driven share-count reduction + margin expansion off a low 2021/22 base, not revenue growth (rev CAGR only +7%, decelerating to +1%) |

**Net read:** the cash-generation and capital-return claims are fully corroborated by the numbers — this is a real FCF machine buying back a fifth of itself. But the *growth* narrative is materially richer than the trajectory justifies: every forward-looking driver management emphasizes (AI search, voice SaaS, Hatch) is being marshalled to explain why a +1% top line will someday reaccelerate, while the core ad business shrinks on paying-location count, clicks, and now even Services.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: Declining (on the core) / Pivoting (on the narrative).**

- **Q1-25 (offensive):** "continued strength," "strong momentum," "excited by the opportunities." Confident, growth-forward.
- **Q2-25 (first defensive notes):** "advertisers exercised increased caution," "did not see the seasonal increase we would typically expect," macro invoked repeatedly. Tone tilts to explaining absence of growth.
- **Q3-25 (defensive + reframing):** "heightened macroeconomic uncertainties will persist," EBITDA *down* 3%, but heavy pivot language — "Yelp's transformation with AI continues to accelerate," 35 new features. Offense rhetorically, defense financially.
- **Q4-25 (full pivot):** the word "transform"/"transformation" dominates; "we plan to increase our investments in 2026." Management is now selling a *future* business.
- **Q1-26 (defensive core, aggressive pivot):** "local businesses have continued to face a challenging economic environment," NI -27%, and the new framing device — a 2028 other-revenue target — to redirect attention away from a +1% quarter and a -11% RR&O. Note the late mention of "the conflict in the Middle East" / "dynamics… in March" as a fresh macro excuse.

The CPC mechanic is a recurring rhetorical shield: clicks fall double-digits, CPC rises double-digits, and management frames this as "delivering fewer but higher-quality clicks" and "advertiser demand outpacing consumer demand." That is partly true (services mix), but it is also how a platform whose *consumer engagement is shrinking* (app unique devices -2% YoY, "consumers visited restaurants with reduced frequency") describes monetizing a declining audience harder.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: High (on hitting/beating the printed guide) — but the guide itself has been serially lowered.**

- The screener records **7/7 quarterly beats (100%)**, and the calls corroborate a consistent beat-and-raise *cadence* on the bottom line: Q1-25 EBITDA "$15M above the high end," Q2-25 "$11M above," Q3-25 "$13M above," Q1-26 EBITDA "$15M above the high end." Management explicitly flows outperformance to EBITDA — David Schwarzbach, Q1-26: "as we've been able to outperform our guidance, we've flowed through that incremental revenue to EBITDA."
- BUT the **full-year revenue guide was cut through the year**: FY25 revenue guided to $1.465–1.485B (Q1-25) → narrowed to $1.465–1.475B (Q2) → *lowered* to $1.460–1.465B (Q3). They beat the *lowered* bar. That is sandbag-and-beat on a declining trajectory, not genuine upside.
- So: **High reliability on the printed number, Medium on the trajectory.** They reliably clear a bar they keep setting lower. The EBITDA beats are real and partly mechanical (capitalized software development was explicitly cited as a Q1-26 EBITDA tailwind — a quality-of-earnings caveat).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"What needs to change for RR&O to stabilize?"** (KeyBanc, Raymond James, Baird recurring). Management's answer never changes: it's "macro," in-restaurant dining "will return," they're "well positioned." Two-plus years of the same answer while RR&O goes from -3% to -11%. The non-answer is the answer: they have no lever on the structural side.
- **OpenAI / data-licensing visibility** (BofA, Q1-26, pointed): "when I search for restaurants on ChatGPT today, Yelp content appears relatively limited versus Reddit, OpenTable, Tripadvisor… what needs to happen for Yelp to become more visible?" Stoppelman *cannot answer* — "we can't really comment on that nor do we have all the details of their plans." The load-bearing AI-distribution story is outside Yelp's control and management admits it.
- **Hatch margin / contribution** (multiple): management deflects to "growth not margin is the focus," and flags that retention payments are being *added back* to adjusted EBITDA — a real quality-of-earnings caution analysts surfaced.
- **Yelp Assistant → actual paid-lead conversion / ROI** (Baird, Q1-26): asked directly whether assistant-driven projects convert and lift advertiser ROI materially differently; answer was qualitative ("we feel really good," "early signs are really good"), no conversion metric. The engagement stat (15% of RaQ projects) is an activity metric, not a revenue metric.

The throughline: analysts keep probing whether the AI/other-revenue story is *incremental and monetizable* or merely *replacing* a shrinking ad base, and management's answers are consistently directional and TAM-flavored rather than quantified.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Paying advertising locations down every quarter** — 517k → 515k → 512k → … → **485k** (Q1-26, -6% YoY). The customer base of the core business is eroding, not just its spend.
🔴 **Services has now stalled** (+1% Q1-26 ad rev) — the one durable growth engine that justified the multiple has joined RR&O in deceleration.
🔴 **App unique devices -2% YoY**, consumers visiting "with reduced frequency" — the consumer flywheel (reviews → traffic → ad value) is going backwards.
🔴 **Net income -27% and adjusted EBITDA -7% in Q1-26** — the pivot is now visibly costing current profitability.
🔴 **Insider open-market activity: 0 buys vs 33 sells (476,663 sh)** — zero real conviction from people who know best; the scan's positive buy_ratio is grant-inflated and false.
🔴 **AI-distribution thesis is outside Yelp's control** — management admits it can't comment on how OpenAI surfaces its content; Reddit/OpenTable/Tripadvisor are ahead in ChatGPT today.
🟢 **Prodigious, opportunistic buyback** — 86M→67M shares (-22%) over four years; $125M in Q1-26 at **$24.58** (near lows), $414M authorization remaining. Management is buying aggressively into weakness, the most credible bull signal in the file.
🟢 **Genuine cash machine** — 90% gross margin, 22% FCF margin, TTM FCF $281M, P/FCF 4.7x, net debt only ~$45M. The balance sheet is clean and the cash is real.
🟢 **Other revenue +75%, Hatch $34M ARR +92%, food ordering +88%** — the new engines are growing fast off a small base; the pivot has *some* traction, not zero.
🟢 **SBC discipline** — SBC/revenue cut to 8% (target <6% by 2027), improving GAAP earnings quality over time.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology migration:** "advertising platform" / "local business" language (2025) gives way to "AI transformation," "conversational," "answers and actions," "essential partner in the AI ecosystem" (2026). When a company restocks its vocabulary faster than its revenue grows, the new words are doing the work the numbers can't.
- **Capital allocation language softened on M&A:** "retaining balance sheet capacity for *potential* acquisitions" (2025) became an actual $270–300M cash deal for Hatch (Feb 2026) — they spent the optionality. The buyback line item also reordered: in Q1-26 capital-allocation priorities are listed as "strategic transactions, traffic acquisition, *then* share repurchases" — buyback dropped to third, a subtle signal they intend to spend more on the pivot and less on the float.
- **"Capitalized software development" repeatedly cited as an EBITDA/margin tailwind** (Q3-25, Q1-26) — more greenfield AI projects → more capitalized cost → flattered adjusted EBITDA. A genuine investment, but it inflates the headline margin the bull case leans on.
- **CFO's 5-year revenue-mix answer** (Q4-25): explicitly guiding investors to expect the mix to shift *away* from ads toward SaaS/licensing/transaction — i.e., management itself is signaling the ad business is not the future growth driver.
- **TCS Capital activism is dormant, not live:** the 2023 sale-push has produced no process; management's own buyback is the de facto "activist" return-of-capital, not a third-party catalyst.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING** (with a STABLE-to-deteriorating core cash engine underneath).

This is a high-FCF, deeply-bought-back local-ad platform whose core advertising business is in slow structural decline (paying locations, clicks, and now even Services all negative), executing a credible-but-unproven pivot into AI SaaS (Hatch, Yelp Host) and data licensing to reaccelerate. The capital allocation itself is shareholder-friendly and disciplined — the 22% share-count reduction at low prices is the real value-creation lever and puts a floor under per-share value even if revenue merely stabilizes.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *Is the core ad business stabilizing at a lower volume (in which case 4.7x FCF + a 22%-and-shrinking share count is a genuine fear-print and the buyback alone re-rates it), or is it in terminal secular decline as Google Maps / Meta / AI-search absorb local discovery (in which case the cheapness is a value trap and "other revenue" is merely treading water against a melting ad base)?* Q1-26 — Services stalling to +1% and consumer devices -2% — tilts the evidence toward the latter being a live risk, not a tail.

**ECONOMIC MOAT:** intangible-brand + network (330M reviews, 8M listings, 500M photos — a genuine first-party local-content asset that AI players are now licensing). But the moat is **ERODING**, not wide: ROIC 9.0% (barely above WACC) and falling, paying-location count declining, audience shrinking, and CPC-up/clicks-down monetization is the signature of harvesting a declining base rather than compounding a growing one. The content corpus has real scarcity value (the OpenAI/Apple/Bing licensing deals prove third parties will pay for it), which is why the moat is eroding rather than gone.

**SECULAR THREAT — material, bordering terminal:** **AI-displacement of local discovery.** Google Maps and Meta already absorb the top of the local-discovery funnel; now generative-AI search (ChatGPT, Gemini) threatens to disintermediate Yelp's consumer destination entirely — and management *admits* Yelp content is currently a minor source in ChatGPT vs Reddit/OpenTable/Tripadvisor. Yelp's counter (license the data to the disruptors) monetizes the corpus but cedes the consumer relationship and caps it at a licensing fee, not an ad-network rent. This is a structurally-shrinking-base risk on the *ad* segment, partially offset by the licensing/SaaS optionality and the buyback. The cheapness is therefore a **hybrid**: a real fear-print on a cash machine with a floor, sitting on a genuinely melting consumer-ad franchise — value, but value-trap-adjacent, which caps the value conviction.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING | MOAT: ERODING | MOAT_TREND: ERODING | SECULAR_THREAT: material

bull_thesis: Yelp is a 90%-gross-margin, ~22%-FCF-margin cash machine at ~5x P/FCF and ~1x EV/sales, pricing terminal decline into a business still generating ~$281M TTM FCF with a 7/7 beat record. Four legs: (1) Cash return outruns erosion — diluted shares cut 22% (86M->67M, 2021-25), fresh $500M buyback (Feb-2026, $414M left = ~28% of float), bought aggressively into weakness ($24.58 avg Q1-26); on $281M FCF Yelp retires ~4-5%+ of shares/yr, so even a flat ad base compounds per-share value (FY-25 EPS +19% on NI +10% — the wedge is the buyback). (2) Diversification — 'other revenue' (Hatch AI lead-mgmt +92%, DoorDash food ordering +88%, AI data licensing incl. OpenAI/Apple/Bing/Meta.ai) grew +75% YoY to $29M/qtr, targeting a $250M run-rate by end-2028 at SaaS margins, valued at ~zero today. (3) AI-licensing option — 330M reviews / 8M listings are scarce trusted local content AI search players need (API calls 20x YoY). (4) Litigation option — Yelp v. Google antitrust suit survived its motion to dismiss Oct-2025, all claims proceeding; a free call on damages/injunctive relief. If the ad base declines no faster than mid-single-digits, the buyback continues, and 'other' scales, a re-rate toward marketplace peers (TRIP ~12-15x, ANGI ~20x) implies a double.
bear_thesis: Yelp's cheapness is the market correctly discounting a melting, AI-disintermediated local-search base, not a mispricing. Same-store decline hides under the headline: paying advertising locations -6% YoY to 485k (517k->485k), ad clicks -10/-11% YoY, and the 'resilient' Services advertising leg collapsed from +14% (Q1-25) to +1% (Q1-26) in a NON-recessionary tape — structural intent-leakage, not a cyclical RR&O dip. RR&O ad revenue -6% FY-25 accelerating to -11% Q1-26. Revenue looks stable only because CPC rose 8-14% YoY on FALLING clicks — scarcity pricing management itself admitted ('fewer clicks... resulted in higher prices,' Q3-25). Secular threat is ai-displacement: Google AI Overviews and ChatGPT answer 'best restaurant near me' inside the result, collapsing the click Yelp monetizes; app unique devices already -2% YoY. The strategic answer — license data to the disintermediators — turns a high-margin retail-ad dollar into a low-margin wholesale penny; asked why Yelp content is 'relatively limited vs Reddit/OpenTable/Tripadvisor' on ChatGPT (Bansal Q1-26), the CEO couldn't answer ('we can't comment'). Catalysts are weak: the antitrust suit is undated, multi-year, single-binary, no damages figure; OpenAI licensing is third-party-controlled/unquantified; the 2023 TCS activist sale-push already failed and exited. Profitability is reversing (adj. EBITDA margin 27%->22%, adj. EBITDA -7%/NI -27% YoY Q1-26) the moment they spent behind Hatch, and the balance sheet flipped from net cash to net DEBT $45M post-Hatch — the 'fortress'/net-cash/138%-MoS inputs are STALE. Insiders sold 476,663 sh with ZERO open-market buys. Confirmation: another quarter of locations decline + Services <=0% + 'other' missing its ramp = value trap where the buyback only slows the bleed.
consensus_delta: Deep-value lens: is the street pricing terminal decline into a company with stable earnings power? Here the street (Hold consensus, ~$29 PT, only +20% upside, analyst-target velocity -11% vs yr-ago) is roughly RIGHT, and the screener's value inputs are the ones that are wrong. The bullish mispricing claim ('138% MoS, net cash, DCF $68') leans on STALE figures: net cash has flipped to net DEBT $45M post-Hatch, and the DCF assumes a durable ad base that is in same-store decline (paying locations -6%, Services growth +14%->+1%, clicks -10%). The genuine consensus delta is narrower and two-sided: the street under-credits the buyback's per-share compounding and the free Yelp-v-Google litigation option, but it correctly prices the structural intent-leakage that management keeps mislabeling as 'macro.' The factually-wrong assumption isn't the street's — it's the screener's premise that Services is a resilient growth engine; Q1-26 (+1%) refutes it. Net: not enough hidden upside to override an eroding base.
forcing_function: No dated, binding forcing function. The candidate events are all SOFT/undated: (1) Yelp v. Google antitrust — survived MTD Oct-2025, all claims proceeding, but in early discovery with NO trial date and NO damages figure (single-binary, multi-year); (2) OpenAI / data-licensing scale-up — third-party-controlled, unquantified, management 'can't comment' on whether Yelp becomes primary; (3) the $250M-other-revenue-by-2028 target — a 2.5-yr soft milestone, not an event; (4) prior activist (TCS Capital) sale-push is FIRED/stale (2023, no deal, exited). Quarterly prints (Q2-26 ~Aug, Q3-26 ~Nov) are the only dated checkpoints, and they are more likely to confirm erosion than force an upward re-rate. Per the regime doc, this is exactly the thesis to deprioritize: it hinges on one shared macro factor (AI-search displacement) with no independent resolution driver.
valley_of_death: Next 3-9 months (Q2-26 8-K early Aug, Q3-26 early Nov): the most likely path is continued deterioration that re-rates DOWN before any bull catalyst fires. Expect another quarter of paying-locations decline (485k trending lower), Services advertising at/near 0% growth, RR&O ad revenue down double-digits, and adj. EBITDA margin pinned near 22% (vs 25-27% in 2025) as Hatch + traffic-acquisition spend runs ahead of its revenue. No debt-maturity wall (net debt only $45M, ample liquidity) and FCF stays positive — the risk is not solvency, it's a guide-down / margin-reset that compresses the multiple further. Management has already flagged the full year: 'we expect the operating environment for local businesses to remain challenging this year.' The buyback cushions but cannot reverse a same-store-revenue decline if Services turns negative.
positioning_washout: Limited mechanical-selling dynamic — no dividend (so no yield-base to flush) and not an index-deletion candidate at ~$1.45B cap. The visible flow is insider selling (33 sells / 476,663 sh, 0 open-market buys) and steady company buyback absorbing float. There is no forced-seller washout to wait for and no over-owned crowded long to unwind; ownership is ordinary institutional + passive. This removes the 'wait for capitulation' (B) setup — there is no identifiable flush event that resets the risk/reward, which pushes the call toward PASS rather than watchlist.
moderator_conclusion: PASS (C), conviction 2. Yelp is a high-quality cash engine (90% gross margin, ~$281M TTM FCF, 22% reduction in share count, 7/7 beats) wrapped around a STRUCTURALLY ERODING local-advertising franchise being disintermediated by AI search. The forensic case is damning where it counts — on the large, current earnings base: Services growth collapsed +14%->+1% in a non-recessionary tape, paying locations -6%, clicks -10%, app devices -2%, RR&O -11% and accelerating, margins reversing (27%->22%), and the screener's headline safety (net cash, 138% MoS, DCF $68) built on STALE inputs that the live balance sheet (net DEBT $45M post-Hatch) and trajectory contradict. The live SoP fair value is ~$33 (range $30-36), well below the screener's $54-68, against a $22.88 price — so there IS a real discount, but it is mostly shared-factor SECTOR BETA (TRIP/ANGI/Yelp all de-rate on the same Google/AI-search tail), not idiosyncratic alpha. The catalysts are all SOFT_EXTENDED or FIRED: the antitrust suit is undated single-binary litigation, the OpenAI/licensing lever is third-party and unquantified, and the activist push already failed in 2023. The current regime (CATALYST_WATCH_REGIME.md) explicitly penalizes theses with no dated forcing function and those hinging on ONE dominant shared macro factor — AI-capex/AI-displacement is THE flagged tail — and favors structural special-sits in distressed/forced-seller lanes, which this is not. With an eroding moat, a material (bordering terminal) ai-displacement threat, no positioning washout to wait for, and no dated re-rate trigger, there is no entry edge here. SEPARATELY, value_conviction = 3 (regime/catalyst-stripped): on valuation vs the ~$33 SoP + forensic quality alone, the stock IS cheap and the FCF/buyback are real — but the ERODING moat + material secular threat CAP value_conviction at 3, because a low multiple on a structurally-shrinking, AI-disintermediated base is a value-trap risk, not durable value. The buyback buys time, not immunity.

The optical 1.5:1+ reward/risk to base is undermined by an ERODING moat and an undated catalyst — the upside requires the AI-displacement thesis to be WRONG, which is the one shared macro tail the regime says to avoid concentrating in.

BUT the discount is shared-factor SECTOR BETA, not idiosyncratic alpha: TRIP, ANGI and Yelp all de-rate together on the same unresolved driver (Google/AI-search disintermediation of consumer review-and-discovery monetization). interrogator_dossier:
# YELP Inc. — Forensic Dossier (Interrogator)

*Six consecutive earnings calls reviewed: Q1-2025 (2025-05-08) through Q1-2026 (2026-05-08). Financials cross-referenced against the screener trajectory (FY2021–FY2025) plus the TTM/latest-quarter overrides (as of 2026-03-31).*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story management told a year ago and the story it tells today are materially different — not in branding, but in what is actually carrying the business.

**Q1-2025 (the high-water mark of the old story):** Yelp opened the period reporting **8% YoY revenue growth** and an expanding margin, led by Services (+14% YoY, "16th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth"). The narrative had two legs: (1) a resilient, high-margin Services advertising engine offsetting a weak Restaurant/Retail & Other (RR&O) book, and (2) AI as an *enhancement* layer — Yelp Assistant driving Request-a-Quote project volume, themed ad formats, better matching. RR&O was a known sore spot (−3% YoY) but framed as cyclical/macro, not structural.

**The deceleration is the spine of the arc.** Watch the headline revenue print degrade call by call: **Q1-25 +8% → Q2-25 +4% → Q3-25 +4% → FY-25 +4% → Q1-26 +1%.** Services advertising, the supposed durable leg, decelerated in lockstep: **+14% (Q1-25) → +8% (Q2-25) → +7% (Q3-25) → +1% (Q1-26).** That last number is the tell — the "resilient" growth engine has converged to flat. RR&O never stopped bleeding: −3% → −5% → −2% (FY −6%) → **−11% in Q1-26**, accelerating *down*, not stabilizing.

**The narrative pivot:** as the ad business flattened, management progressively re-pointed the story at three new pillars introduced and amplified across the year — (a) **AI SaaS products** (Yelp Host call-answering for restaurants, Yelp Receptionist for services, now folded into the **Hatch** acquisition closed Feb-2026), (b) **data licensing** to AI search players (the "$10M run-rate" disclosed Q2-25, then the **OpenAI** agreement headlined Q4-25/Q1-26), and (c) **transaction revenue** via the **DoorDash** food-ordering partnership. By Q1-26 these are bundled as "**other revenue**," which grew **+75% YoY to $29M** — and management has set a flag in the ground: a **$250M other-revenue run-rate target by end-2028**, "more than double" the Q1-26 run-rate.

So the arc is a classic incumbent-in-transition: the core monetization engine (CPC advertising on local-business listings) is decelerating toward zero growth, and a small, fast-growing, lower-visibility set of adjacencies is being promoted to carry the equity story. The question the rest of this dossier interrogates: **is the new leg real and large enough, or is it a $29M tail being asked to do the work of a $1.4B body that is quietly shrinking on a same-store basis?**

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Where stated | The hard numbers | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Record net revenue… strong profitability" | Q2/Q3-25, FY-25 | FY-25 rev $1.46B (+4%), NI $146M (+10%), EPS $2.24 (+19%) | **True but flattering.** Record revenue is real; but +4% is the *slowest* growth in the 5-yr table (rev CAGR was +7.1%). EPS growth is buyback-driven, not operational. |
| Services is the durable growth engine | every call | Services ad rev +14%→+8%→+7%→**+1%** (Q1-26) | **Decisively breaking.** The "resilient" leg decelerated 14pts in four quarters to flat. The thesis pillar is failing in real time. |
| Margin potential of the business (24–27% adj. EBITDA) | Q1–Q3-25 | Adj. EBITDA margin 24%→27%→26% in 2025, but **22% in Q1-26**; adj. EBITDA −7% YoY, NI −27% YoY Q1-26 | **Reversing.** The margin story peaked mid-2025 and is now contracting as they spend behind Hatch + traffic acquisition. |
| EPS growth is strong (+19% YoY) | FY-25 | Diluted shares cut 86M→67M (−22%) 2021–25; FY-25 EPS +19% | **Engineered, not earned.** Net income +10% but EPS +19% — the gap is the buyback. TTM diluted EPS has already rolled to **$2.18** (below the $2.24 FY print), and Q1-26 latest-Q EPS was **−17% YoY**. The per-share tailwind is decelerating as the price (and thus shares retired per dollar) matters less than the shrinking numerator. |
| CPC strength shows pricing power | Q2/Q3-25, Q1-26 | CPC +9%→+11%→+14%→+8% YoY; ad clicks −3%→−7%→−11%→−10% YoY | **A red flag dressed as strength.** Rising CPC on *falling* clicks is mix/scarcity, not demand. Management itself admits (Q3-25, Schwarzbach) "fewer clicks on the platform… resulted in higher prices." Revenue per click is propping up a platform with shrinking engagement. |
| FCF generation is strong (P/FCF 4.6x) | implied by screener | FY FCF margin 22%; **TTM FCF $281M**; net debt now **+$45M** (was net cash) | **FCF real, but the balance sheet flipped.** The screener's "−299M net debt" (net cash) is STALE. Post-Hatch ($271M cash), the recomputed 2026-03-31 position is **net DEBT $45M** (total debt $155M − cash $110M). The "fortress balance sheet" leg of the value case is materially weaker than the input headline. |
| Other revenue is the future ($250M by 2028) | Q1-26 | Other rev $29M in Q1-26 (~$116M annualized run-rate); +75% YoY | **Real growth, trivial base.** Even if they hit $250M run-rate by end-2028, that is ~$130M of *incremental* revenue spread over ~3 years against a $1.46B base that is flat-to-down. It does not offset a shrinking ad core on any near-term horizon. |
| Paying locations stable | management de-emphasizes | 517k → 515k → 512k → **485k** (Q1-26) | **Quiet erosion accelerating.** −6% YoY in Q1-26 — the customer *count* is leaving, masked by per-location CPC inflation. This is the single most damning operating metric. |

**Bottom line of the forensic:** the screener's value headline (P/FCF 4.6x, 138% margin of safety, net cash, beat 7/7) is built substantially on **stale and backward-looking inputs**. The live picture — flat revenue, decelerating Services, −11% RR&O, −6% paying locations, margin contraction, a balance sheet that flipped to net debt, and TTM EPS already rolling over — is a business whose *current* trajectory is decelerating into the AI-search transition, not a stable cash machine being mispriced for no reason.

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING (offensive → defensive).**

- **Q1-25 — confident, offensive:** "Led by continued strength in our Services business… 8% growth and strong profitability." Forward-leaning AI-product language ("reinvent the search experience," "take it off of Yelp" as an API). Guidance *raised/widened upward*.
- **Q2-25 — hedging creeps in:** "advertisers exercised increased caution… we did not see the seasonal increase in revenue that we would typically expect." Still record-revenue framing, but the macro caveat moves from footnote to thesis. Narrows full-year revenue range.
- **Q3-25 — defensive, macro-anchored:** RR&O weakness now attributed flatly to "macro… continues to be uncertainty"; Jed Nachman: "the main story there is the macro… at the margins, we see impacts from some of the competitive pressures." Note the *deflection* — competition acknowledged only "at the margins." Full-year revenue range **lowered**.
- **FY-25 / Q1-26 — pivot-and-promise:** the tone shifts from defending the ad business to *selling a new story* — Hatch, Yelp Host, OpenAI, the $250M-by-2028 other-revenue target. This is the classic move: when the core decelerates, management lengthens the horizon and points at a TAM ("over $1 billion in the United States for Yelp Host"). Q1-26: "we expect the operating environment for local businesses to remain challenging this year" — an explicit defensive admission for the full year ahead.

The confidence is being *relocated* from delivered ad results (which are deteriorating) to undelivered AI optionality (which is unproven at scale). That is a declining-confidence signature even though the delivery cadence ("beat guidance again") stays intact.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH on the short-cycle beat mechanics, but the bar is being lowered.**

Yelp has a genuine, repeatable habit of setting conservative quarterly ranges and beating them — corroborated by the screener's **beat 7/7 (100%)** and visible in the transcripts: Q1-25 revenue "$4M above the high end," adj. EBITDA "$15M above the high end"; Q2-25 "$3M above"/"$11M above"; Q3-25 "$6M above"/"$13M above"; Q1-26 "$6M above"/"$15M above." The EBITDA beats are large and consistent — there is a clear **sandbagging pattern** on the bottom line (they guide cautious opex, then flow outperformance through).

**But two credibility erosions matter:**
1. **The full-year revenue guide was cut intra-year** (Q3-25 lowered to $1.460–1.465B from $1.465–1.485B). Beating a quarterly number you set low while cutting the annual number is not the same as delivering — it is managing the cadence while the trajectory slips.
2. **The 2025 SBC target ("less than 8% by end-2025") was hit (8% in Q1-26), but the next leg ("less than 6% by end-2027") is the load-bearing promise for GAAP profitability — undelivered.** And the EBITDA-margin "potential" narrative (24–27%) was directly contradicted by the Q1-26 print (22%, −7% YoY) the moment they started spending behind Hatch. Management's own margin guidance proved aspirational the first quarter it was tested against real investment.

So: **High** reliability on "we will beat this quarter's modest bar"; **Medium** on "the trajectory is what we imply" — they sandbag the print and lower the destination.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

The Q&A reveals exactly where the bodies are, by what management *won't* answer crisply:

- **"What's really driving services deceleration — is it competition or macro?"** (Segura, Q2-25; Witaszek/Baird, Q2-25). Management reflexively answers "macro," repeatedly, and confines competition (food-delivery providers eating restaurants; Google eating local search) to "at the margins." This is the **central deflection** — every quarter analysts probe whether the decline is structural; every quarter management insists it is cyclical. The −11% RR&O and −6% locations in Q1-26 are the bad news that probe foreshadowed.
- **CPC-up / clicks-down** (multiple quarters): analysts repeatedly ask whether rising CPC is healthy. Schwarzbach's Q3-25 answer is candid but unflattering — fewer clicks *cause* higher prices. Analysts are circling the fact that revenue stability is an artifact of scarcity pricing, not demand.
- **OpenAI partnership scope** (Bansal/BofA, Q1-26): the sharpest exchange. Bansal points out that on ChatGPT today "**Yelp content appears relatively limited versus sources like Reddit, OpenTable, Tripadvisor**" and asks what makes Yelp primary. Jeremy *cannot answer*: "we can't really comment on… their plans… continue to watch that space." The load-bearing AI-licensing catalyst is one where Yelp has **no control and no visibility** into whether it becomes material.
- **Hatch margin/contribution** (Patel/Raymond James, Q4-25; Bansal, Q1-26): analysts press for the EBITDA impact and contribution; Schwarzbach declines to quantify margin ("driving margin is not the immediate focus") and notes retention payments are *added back* to adjusted EBITDA — a quality-of-earnings caution.

What analysts worry about that management isn't addressing: **whether AI search (Google AI Overviews + ChatGPT) structurally disintermediates Yelp's role as the destination for local discovery** — i.e., whether Yelp becomes a wholesale data supplier (licensing pennies) instead of the retail advertising venue (CPC dollars). Management's answer is to *embrace* the licensing role as upside, but never reconciles it against the cannibalization of the far larger ad business.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Services advertising decelerated from +14% to +1% YoY in four quarters** — the durable-growth pillar is collapsing (Q1-25→Q1-26).
🔴 **Paying advertising locations −6% YoY to 485k** (517k→485k across the year) — customers are leaving; revenue held by CPC inflation, not retention (Q1-26).
🔴 **Ad clicks −10% to −11% YoY** while CPC +8–14% — engagement/traffic is structurally falling; "pricing power" is scarcity pricing (Q2-25→Q1-26).
🔴 **Margin reversed: adj. EBITDA margin 27% (Q2-25) → 22% (Q1-26), adj. EBITDA −7% YoY, NI −27% YoY** the moment they invested behind Hatch (Q1-26).
🔴 **Balance sheet flipped from net cash to net debt $45M** post-Hatch — the "fortress" value-input is stale (2026-03-31).
🔴 **Insider behavior: 0 open-market buys vs 33 sells (476,663 sh)** — no insider is buying the "138% margin of safety."
🔴 **App unique devices −2% YoY** (FY-25) — the consumer funnel itself is shrinking, not just monetization.
🔴 **The load-bearing AI catalysts (OpenAI, data licensing) are third-party-controlled and unquantified** — management explicitly "can't comment" on whether Yelp content becomes primary (Q1-26).

🟢 **Buyback is aggressive and accretive:** diluted shares 86M→67M (−22%, 2021–25); $500M authorization added Feb-2026; repurchasing through the price weakness ($32–37 avg). Real per-share value transfer.
🟢 **Gross margin 90% and FCF margin ~22%** — the unit economics of the legacy platform remain excellent; this is a cash machine, not a cash incinerator.
🟢 **Other revenue +75% YoY** with a credible (if small) diversification roadmap (Hatch +92%, food ordering +88%, licensing) — genuine optionality, real product velocity.
🟢 **Beat 7/7 with large EBITDA beats** — execution discipline and conservative guidance are real.

The flags are asymmetric: the red flags hit the *core, large, current* earnings base; the green flags are either financial engineering (buyback) or a *small, future* adjacency. That asymmetry is the whole investment question.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology migration: "advertising platform" → "AI transformation."** Across the six calls the self-description shifts from a local-advertising marketplace to "transforming Yelp with AI." When a decelerating incumbent re-labels itself around the very technology threatening it, that is both an offensive bet and a tell that the old label no longer sells.
- **"Other revenue" is being promoted from a footnote to a headline.** A year ago it was barely mentioned; by Q1-26 it has a multi-year run-rate target ($250M by 2028) and leads the prepared remarks. Management is teaching the street to value the stub, not the core — because the core is shrinking.
- **The data-licensing framing is double-edged and management knows it.** They sell OpenAI/Apple/Bing/Meta.ai licensing as validation ("essential partner"). But every one of those surfaces is a venue where a consumer gets the answer *without visiting Yelp* — i.e., the licensing revenue is the toll Yelp collects on its own disintermediation. Jeremy's repeated "we're before the first inning" hedge is doing a lot of work to avoid sizing the cannibalization.
- **Capital-allocation language tightened around acquisitions.** Earlier calls list "returning excess capital" prominently; by FY-25/Q1-26 the first priority becomes "investing in strategic transactions" (Hatch) — a subtle reordering from shareholder return toward empire-building into the AI-SaaS adjacency, where Yelp has no proven edge against far better-capitalized players (a risk Bansal flagged directly in Q1-26).
- **No management changes**, but a notable *constancy*: founder-CEO Stoppelman has run this for ~20 years through every prior "reinvent the search experience" promise. The pattern of perennial product reinvention without revenue inflection is itself a signal.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: DETERIORATING / PIVOTING.** The core local-advertising franchise is in same-store decline (paying locations −6%, Services growth collapsed to +1%, RR&O −11%, clicks −10%), masked at the headline by CPC scarcity-pricing and an aggressive buyback. Management is mid-pivot into AI-SaaS (Hatch) and data licensing — real product velocity, but a base too small ($29M/qtr) and a competitive position too exposed to offset the erosion of the ad core on any horizon the buyback can bridge.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *Is the cheapness (P/FCF ~5x) a fear-print mispricing of a stable cash machine, or the market correctly discounting a structurally-shrinking local-search franchise being disintermediated by Aic search?* The evidence here leans toward the latter on the **operating trajectory**, partially offset by a still-excellent FCF/buyback that can return capital faster than the base erodes for some years. This is a *melting-ice-cube-with-a-good-air-conditioner* situation, not a clean mispricing — and the input headline ("net cash, 138% MoS, beat 7/7") is built on stale figures that overstate the safety.

**ECONOMIC MOAT.** Moat type: **intangible-brand + two-sided network** (330M reviews, 8M listings, consumer mindshare for "find a local business"). Historically a real moat. **WIDTH judged by the evidence: NARROW and ERODING.** ROIC is **9.0%** — barely above cost of capital, and *falling* as margins compress (adj. EBITDA margin 27%→22%, NI margin 12%→5% Q1-26); ad clicks and unique devices are declining (−10%, −2%), meaning the network's *consumer* side — the source of the moat — is thinning. A moat whose underlying engagement is shrinking and whose returns are compressing is not wide; it is eroding. **MOAT_TREND: ERODING.**

**SECULAR THREAT — TERMINAL vs cyclical.** The threat is **ai-displacement / AI-search disintermediation of local discovery**, and it is **MATERIAL, bordering on terminal.** Google AI Overviews and ChatGPT answer "best taco near me" *in the result*, collapsing the click-through that Yelp monetizes; Yelp's own consumer funnel (unique devices −2%, clicks −10%) is already showing it. Management's strategic response — license the data to the disintermediators — converts a high-margin retail-ad dollar into a low-margin wholesale-licensing penny, a structurally lower-value terminal state even if it "works."
